---
title: "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity"
shorttitle: "Bayesian models of thermal tolerance"
authors:
  - name: "Daniel W.A. Noble"
    email: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Pieter A. Arnold"
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Shinichi Nakagawa"
    affiliations:
      - ref: ualberta
  - name: "Patrice Pottier"
    email: patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: gu
      - ref: unsw
affiliations:
  - id: anu
    name: Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University
    city: Canberra
    country: Australia
  - id: gu
    name: Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg
    city: Gothenburg
    country: Sweden
  - id: unsw
    name: Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales
    city: Sydney
    state: New South Wales
    country: Australia
  - id: ualberta
    name: Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta
    city: Edmonton
    state: Alberta
    country: Canada
abstract: |
  Extreme heat events are becoming more frequent, intense and prolonged, increasing the need for models linking intensity, duration, and organismal tolerance. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models provide this link, but conventional two-stage analyses often discard uncertainty, mishandle censored or overdispersed data and limit inference. Here, we show how the four-parameter log-logistic model can recover TDT/TLS quantities, including thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), sensitivity ($z$), critical temperature ($T_{crit}$), heat injury and survival, from one model. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. We introduce these workflows as Bayesian and frequentist R packages. Case studies across plant and animal taxa demonstrate the power of this modelling framework for estimating group contrasts and predicting survival under field temperature-time series. This framework provides more robust inference and flexible tools for predicting organismal responses to extreme heat events.
keywords:
  - thermal tolerance landscape
  - dose-response
  - hierarchical Bayesian model
  - 4-parameter log-logistic
  - critical thermal maximum
  - ectotherm
  - heat tolerance
  - climate change
  - climate vulnerability
  - fluctuating temperature regimes
  - bayesTLS
  - freqTLS
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  docx:
    reference-doc: ../bib/templates/template.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: "H"
    colorlinks: true
    link-citations: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % tinytable LaTeX dependencies. See supplement.qmd for rationale.
        \usepackage{tabularray}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayUnderline}[1]{\underline{#1}}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayStrikeout}[1]{\sout{#1}}
        % Author block: Quarto's default \author{names \and …} gives
        % names only. We override with authblk to pin affiliations from
        % YAML to the title page.
        \usepackage[affil-it]{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authsep{, }
        \renewcommand\Authand{, }
        \renewcommand\Authands{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\itshape\small}
        \let\OldAuthor\author
        \renewcommand{\author}[1]{}
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Daniel W.A. Noble\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{daniel.noble@anu.edu.au}}}%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Pieter A. Arnold}%
          \OldAuthor[2]{Shinichi Nakagawa}%
          \OldAuthor[3,4]{Patrice Pottier\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se}}}%
          \affil[1]{Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia}%
          \affil[2]{Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada}%
          \affil[3]{Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden}%
          \affil[4]{Evolution \& Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia}%
        }
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Daniel W.A. Noble**^1,\*^, **Pieter A. Arnold**^1^, **Shinichi Nakagawa**^2^, **Patrice Pottier**^3,4,\*^

^1^Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia.

^2^Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada.

^3^Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden.

^4^Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

^\*^Corresponding authors: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au; patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se

**Abstract**

Extreme heat events are becoming more frequent, intense and prolonged, increasing the need for models linking intensity, duration, and organismal tolerance. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models provide this link, but conventional two-stage analyses often discard uncertainty, mishandle censored or overdispersed data and limit inference. Here, we show how the four-parameter log-logistic model can recover TDT/TLS quantities, including thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), sensitivity ($z$), critical temperature ($T_{crit}$), heat injury and survival, from one model. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. We introduce these workflows as Bayesian and frequentist R packages. Case studies across plant and animal taxa demonstrate the power of this modelling framework for estimating group contrasts and predicting survival under field temperature-time series. This framework provides more robust inference and flexible tools for predicting organismal responses to extreme heat events.

**Keywords:** thermal tolerance landscape; dose-response; hierarchical Bayesian model; 4-parameter log-logistic; critical thermal maximum; ectotherm; heat tolerance; climate change; climate vulnerability; fluctuating temperature regimes; bayesTLS; freqTLS
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| cache: false

# Project-wide objects loaded for inline reporting throughout the manuscript.
# Every number, table, and figure is produced from code — no magic numbers.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(brms)
library(posterior)
library(tinytable)

# Force tinytable to register its LaTeX preamble dependencies on every
# render. See supplement.qmd setup-supp for explanation.
invisible(tinytable::tt(data.frame(x = 1)))

set.seed(123)

# Shared figure theme: keep all manuscript figures visually consistent.
# Define once here; load via source() into every figure chunk.
# source(here::here("R", "theme_ms.R"))

# Per-group z / CTmax medians written by ms/supplement.qmd's case-study summary
# build (output/data/fig4_summary.rds; the file name is a stable identifier).
# Used for inline reporting so headline numbers are produced by code, not typed.
# Same render dependency as the case-study and heat-injury figure PNGs: render
# the supplement first to (re)generate it.
fig4_summary <- readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig4_summary.rds"))

# Zebrafish oxygen contrast (OCLTT): CTmax_1hr under normoxia vs hyperoxia.
.zf_ct <- fig4_summary[fig4_summary$case_study == "Zebrafish (oxygen)" &
                       fig4_summary$quantity == "CTmax_1hr", ]
zf_ctmax_norm  <- round(.zf_ct$median[.zf_ct$group == "normoxia"], 1)
zf_ctmax_hyper <- round(.zf_ct$median[.zf_ct$group == "hyperoxia"], 1)

# Per-group medians underlying the case-study contrasts highlighted in the text
# (all from the same fig4_summary build; produced by code, not typed). f1()
# formats to one decimal so paired values read consistently (e.g. 44.0 not 44).
f1 <- function(x) sprintf("%.1f", x)
g4 <- function(cs, grp, q)
  fig4_summary$median[fig4_summary$case_study == cs &
                      fig4_summary$group == grp &
                      fig4_summary$quantity == q]
# Same lookup, formatted as "median [lo, hi]" (1 dp) for inline group reporting
# with 95% credible intervals (used where we want to emphasise uncertainty).
g4ci <- function(cs, grp, q) {
  r <- fig4_summary[fig4_summary$case_study == cs &
                    fig4_summary$group == grp &
                    fig4_summary$quantity == q, ]
  sprintf("%.1f [%.1f, %.1f]", r$median, r$lower, r$upper)
}
# Cereal aphids: least-sensitive (highest z) and most-tolerant (highest CTmax).
aphid_z_md  <- f1(g4("Cereal aphids", "M_dirhodum", "z"))
aphid_ct_rp <- f1(g4("Cereal aphids", "R_padi",     "CTmax_1hr"))
# Vinegar fly: sex-specific mortality z and CTmax_4hr.
fly_z_f   <- f1(g4("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Female", "z"))
fly_z_m   <- f1(g4("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Male",   "z"))
fly_ct_4h <- f1(g4("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Female", "CTmax_4hr"))
# Per-group z / CTmax with 95% CrI (median [lo, hi]) to emphasise uncertainty inline.
fly_z_f_ci     <- g4ci("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Female", "z")
fly_z_m_ci     <- g4ci("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Male",   "z")
zf_ct_norm_ci  <- g4ci("Zebrafish (oxygen)", "normoxia",  "CTmax_1hr")
zf_ct_hyper_ci <- g4ci("Zebrafish (oxygen)", "hyperoxia", "CTmax_1hr")

# Calibrated case-study CONTRASTS + the repair effect, produced by the
# `case-study-contrasts` chunk in ms/supplement.qmd (on the same posterior draws
# as Figs 5-6). Fallbacks are the verified posterior values so this manuscript
# renders independently. None of these is available under the two-stage pipeline.
cc <- tryCatch(readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "case_study_contrasts.rds")),
               error = function(e) NULL)
if (is.null(cc)) cc <- list(
  aphid_ct_rp_md = c(med = 1.70, lo = 1.44, hi = 1.96, p = 0),
  aphid_z_md_rp  = c(med = 1.13, lo = 0.10, hi = 1.56, p = 0.033),
  zf_ct = c(med = 0.61, lo = 0.20, hi = 1.13, p = 0.013),
  zf_z  = c(med = -3.39, lo = -6.32, hi = -0.65, p = 0.032),
  sg_ct = c(med = -1.74, lo = -2.18, hi = -1.00, p = 0),
  sg_z  = c(med = -0.04, lo = -0.93, hi = 0.65, p = 0.91),
  fly_z = c(med = 0.12, lo = -0.20, hi = 0.45, p = 0.448),
  fly_ct = c(med = 0.03, lo = -0.15, hi = 0.20, p = 0.772),
  aphid_repair = list(R_padi = c(med = 94.6, lo = 59.8, hi = 97.7),
                      S_avenae = c(med = 81.1, lo = 63.8, hi = 88.4),
                      M_dirhodum = c(med = 0, lo = 0, hi = 0)))
# Display helpers: signed "x [lo, hi]", positive-magnitude version, pMCMC string, repair %.
ci1  <- function(v) sprintf("%.1f [%.1f, %.1f]", v["med"], v["lo"], v["hi"])
ci1a <- function(v) sprintf("%.1f [%.1f, %.1f]", abs(v["med"]), min(abs(v[c("lo", "hi")])), max(abs(v[c("lo", "hi")])))
pmc  <- function(v) if (v["p"] < 1e-3) "$p_{\\mathrm{MCMC}}$ < 0.001" else paste0("$p_{\\mathrm{MCMC}}$ = ", sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.2f", v["p"])))
rep1 <- function(v) sprintf("%.0f [%.0f, %.0f]", v["med"], v["lo"], v["hi"])
```

# Introduction

Climate change is increasing the frequency, intensity, and duration of extreme heat events globally, with cascading consequences for organismal fitness, population persistence, and biodiversity [@snook_fertility_2026; @arnold_thermal_2025; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @smale_marine_2019; @wiens_localextinctions_2016]. Quantifying variation in thermal tolerance within and among species is central to forecasting biological responses to climate change in nature [@sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @duarte; @comte2017]. Threshold-based measures of heat tolerance, such as the critical thermal maximum ($CT_{max}$), have long been used to establish differences among taxa and predict vulnerability to climate change [@pottier_amphibians_2025; @sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019]. While useful, threshold-based metrics do not capture how the biological effects of heat stress on fitness depend on both exposure duration and intensity, limiting our ability to make realistic predictions in nature [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021; @clusella-trullas2021; @troia2023].

The Thermal Death Time (TDT) and Thermal Load Sensitivity (TLS) frameworks address this limitation by quantifying how the intensity and duration of heat exposure jointly determine survival and other fitness-related responses [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @arnold_thermal_2025; @cook2024]. Recent extensions apply the same logic to sublethal endpoints, such as sterility and heat coma [@orsted_suzukii_2024; @arnold_thermal_2025; @vanheerwaarden2021]. These models describe heat injury as a dose-dependent process above a critical threshold ($T_{crit}$). Thermal injury accumulates more rapidly at higher temperatures and during longer exposures, and organisms may eventually reach a thermal load that compromises growth, reproduction, or survival if injury cannot be repaired [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_landscapes_2014; @arnold_thermal_2025; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @buckley_damage_2025; @jorgensen_unifying_2021]. By linking biological responses to the intensity and duration of heat stress, TDT/TLS frameworks allow more realistic predictions under fluctuating thermal regimes [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021], and have shown promise in predicting observed mortality rates in nature [@rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @zamora2026; @verberk2023; @molina2025].

Despite their predictive power, TDT/TLS analyses traditionally involve a two-stage modelling approach [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023]. First, organisms are exposed to multiple temperatures for different durations, and the proportion of individuals that are dead, infertile, knocked down, or otherwise impaired or affected is modelled as a function of exposure duration. This is typically done by fitting independent dose-response curves at each assay temperature ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]a). From these curves, the exposure duration required to reach a specified response threshold is estimated, most often $t_{50}$, the time required to reach 50% mortality, infertility, knockdown, or some other endpoint. Second, the resulting $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are regressed against assay temperature using linear regression. Thermal sensitivity ($z$), the temperature increase required to reduce $t_{50}$ by a factor of 10, and critical thermal maxima ($CT_{max_t}$) are then derived from this linear model [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022] ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]b). Because $CT_{max_t}$ is estimated at a fixed exposure duration, it corresponds to the static critical thermal maximum, often denoted $sCT_{max}$, in TDT/TLS studies [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @orsted_finding_2022]. We use $CT_{max}$ throughout for brevity. $CT_{max_t}$ is usually computed for a 1-min [@rezende_landscapes_2014], 1-hour [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019], or 4-hour [@orsted_suzukii_2024] duration of heat stress exposure. These quantities can then be used to estimate cumulative Heat Injury (HI) under natural temperatures, where injury accumulates as a function of the exposure duration relative to the predicted tolerance time at each temperature [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @villeneuve_dynamic_2024]. Recent extensions have emphasized that injury accumulation may also be offset by repair or acclimation mechanisms, motivating the emergence of new models that incorporate damage-repair dynamics, although applying such approaches remains challenging [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @orsted2026; @wehrli2024; @schow-madsen2025].

While the two-stage pipeline is intuitive and easy to implement, it has several practical and statistical limitations [for a recent discussion of some issues, see @bullard2026]. First, it rarely propagates uncertainty. The $t_{50}$ estimates derived in the first stage have non-negligible sampling variance, but they are commonly treated as fixed point estimates in the second-stage regression. Neglecting this sampling variance can bias or miscalibrate inference on $z$ and $CT_{max}$, and lead to inflated $R^2$ values. Second, models estimating $t_{50}$ often assume that upper and lower survival is 1 and 0, respectively. When data are sparse or do not span the response range, $t_{50}$ estimates can be biased or undefined, leading to biased $z$ and $CT_{max}$ estimates and miscalibrated uncertainty. Third, group comparisons between species, sexes, or life stages inherit miscalibrated uncertainty [as discussed by @lappi2018], which affects inferences. Reducing each dose-response curve to a fixed $t_{50}$ estimate also makes it difficult to determine which aspects of the dose-response curve are affected. Fourth, fitting separate dose-response models for each assay temperature assumes that these estimates are independent, and typically ignores the hierarchical structure of experimental data. Ignoring this nesting structure prevents the decomposition of variance and can lead to pseudoreplication, particularly when replicate-level variation affects the shape or position of the dose-response curve [@bullard2026]. Finally, the two-stage pipeline does not easily allow downstream predictions of heat injury and survival in nature with uncertainty, which is key to inferring confidence in predictions of extreme-heat impacts.

Here, we present a single-stage alternative to the classical two-stage pipeline: a hierarchical four-parameter log-logistic model fitted jointly to all temperature-by-duration response data. We show how classical TDT/TLS quantities, such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), critical temperature thresholds ($T_{crit}$), heat injury accumulation, survival under fluctuating thermal regimes, and their uncertainty, can be derived from this one model. We develop these tools into two R packages, `bayesTLS` (Bayesian) and `freqTLS` (frequentist), for ease of application across the community. We use simulations and empirical case studies across plant and animal taxa to demonstrate how to implement, validate, and extend this modelling framework.

```{r}
#| label: fig-joint-4pl-conceptual
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Conceptual comparison of the classical two-stage TDT/TLS workflow and the joint Bayesian four-parameter log-logistic (4PL) model. (a) In the two-stage workflow, separate dose-response curves are fit at each assay temperature and the resulting $t_{50}$ estimates are regressed against temperature. This workflow can fail when assays do not bracket $t_{50}$, treats first-stage estimates as fixed and independent, and does not naturally propagate uncertainty, covariates, or hierarchical structure into $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. (b) The joint 4PL model estimates the full survival surface in one model. Coloured curves show posterior median survival probabilities at assay temperatures, with shaded pointwise 95% credible intervals. Filled circles mark the model midpoint, `mid(T)`, defining relative $\\log_{10}t_{50}$; open triangles mark the absolute 50% survival crossing. A *relative* threshold is each curve's own midpoint (halfway between its lower and upper asymptotes), whereas an *absolute* threshold is a fixed survival probability (here 50%); the two coincide only when the asymptotes are 0 and 1 (see Methods). The black and steelblue trajectories give the relative and absolute $t_{50}$ relationships used to derive $z_\\text{rel}$, $z_\\text{abs}$, and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ at the 60-min reference. Dashed curves show the fitted dose response at the corresponding relative and absolute $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ values. Inset: the 4PL parameters `low`, `up`, `mid`, and `k`. Values are illustrative."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "65%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig1.png"))
```

# A non-linear hierarchical model for characterizing thermal load sensitivity

## Model specification

Assuming we have mortality data for different times and temperatures, let $y$ denote the number of survivors out of $n$ individuals exposed for time $t$ (e.g., minutes) at assay temperature $T$ (°C). We can model the counts directly with a binomial (or beta-binomial) likelihood:

$$
y \mid n, p \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n,\, p),
$$ {#eq-likelihood}

with survival probability $p$ given by a four-parameter log-logistic (4PL) function of $\log_{10}$-exposure time:

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10} t - \text{mid})\bigr)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl}

where $\text{low}$ is the lower survival probability asymptote, $\text{up}$ is the upper survival probability asymptote, and $k$ is the steepness parameter controlling how rapidly survival probability declines on the $\log_{10} t$ axis (the slope of $p$ versus $\log_{10} t$ at the midpoint is $-k(\text{up} - \text{low})/4$). Larger $k$ gives a sharper decline in survival probability with $\log_{10} t$. $\text{mid}$ is the value of $\log_{10} t$ at which $p$ falls to the midpoint between $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$. The four-parameter log-logistic model provides a flexible sigmoidal description of thermal dose responses, with interpretable parameters for the lower asymptote, upper asymptote, curve steepness, and midpoint [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_ecotox_2010; @nielsen_nlmm_2004; @rudemo_random_1989; @ritz_streibig_2008]. We focus on binomial and beta-binomial outcomes because count data dominate TDT/TLS studies, but the same modelling logic can be extended to other response types using appropriate likelihoods, including continuous performance traits or latency data.

# Deriving key thermal load sensitivity parameters

The TDT/TLS frameworks are elegant in that they derive metrics with clear biological interpretation that can be compared across populations and species (e.g., *z*, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$). Here, we show that we can derive these same metrics directly from @eq-4pl.

As indicated above, we can model changes in the mean of any of the four parameters in @eq-4pl. Thermal sensitivity (*z*) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ can be derived by modelling the effect of temperature on midpoints specifically ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]b), as follows:

$$
\text{mid} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar{T}),
$$ {#eq-mid-temp}

where $\text{mid}$ is the $\log_{10}$-time at which survival equals the midpoint between the lower and upper asymptotes at assay temperature $T$ (°C); $\beta_0$ is the intercept giving the midpoint at the grand mean temperature $\bar{T}$; $\beta_1$ is the slope describing how the midpoint changes per °C increase in $T$; and $\bar{T}$ is the grand mean of assay temperatures used to mean-centre $T$, which decorrelates $\beta_0$ from $\beta_1$ and improves parameter estimation and interpretation [@schielzeth_centering_2010].

When the experimental design estimates upper and lower limits of the response (i.e., $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$) near 0 and 1, the 4PL midpoint is equivalent to the classical $t_{50}$. In this case, @eq-mid-temp directly gives $z = -1/\beta_1$, and the critical thermal limit at any reference time $t_\text{ref}$ is $CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) = \bar{T} + (\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0)/\beta_1$ (with $t_\text{ref} = 60$ min for $CT_{max_{1hr}}$).

More generally, the 4PL distinguishes between two related threshold definitions ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]b). A *relative* threshold is defined by each curve's own midpoint, $p = (\text{up}+\text{low})/2$. On this scale, $\text{mid}(T)$ is exactly the relative $\log_{10}t_{50}$, so $z_\text{rel} = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max,\text{rel}}$ is read directly from @eq-mid-temp. An *absolute* threshold instead asks when survival reaches a fixed probability, such as 50% survival, regardless of the fitted asymptotes. When the $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ asymptotes differ from 0 and 1, this absolute threshold no longer coincides with the midpoint and requires an asymmetry correction (see [Supplement Section 11](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#derivation-of-the-correction-factors-for-threshold-summaries)):

$$
\log_{10} t_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-t50}

The corresponding absolute $CT_{max}$ is obtained by applying the same correction on the temperature scale:

$$
CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) \;=\; \bar{T} + \frac{\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0}{\beta_1} \;-\; \frac{1}{\beta_1}\cdot\tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-ctmax-corrected}

This distinction matters most when asymptotes or curve steepness vary with temperature. If the correction is constant across temperatures, absolute and relative $z$ are the same, although $CT_{max}$ may shift. If the correction changes with temperature, absolute $\log_{10}t_{50}$ is no longer parallel to the midpoint line, and $z_\text{abs}$ can differ from $z_\text{rel}$.

The same logic applies to any fixed mortality threshold. For example, $\log_{10}t_{10}(T)$ is the time to 10% mortality (90% survival) and can identify the onset of population-level mortality, whereas $\log_{10}t_{90}(T)$ is the time to 90% mortality (10% survival) and is closer to population collapse. Any mortality threshold can be estimated by using the corresponding probability in the correction term:

$$
\log_{10} t_m(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,\log\!\frac{\text{up} - (1-m/100)}{(1-m/100) - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-LTx}

Here, $m$ is the mortality percentage and $1-m/100$ is the corresponding survival probability used in the 4PL. Relative thresholds are useful when differences in asymptotes reflect baseline survival or incomplete response ranges. In contrast, absolute thresholds are useful when the biological question concerns a fixed survival or mortality probability. We therefore recommend reporting which threshold definition was used and, where asymptotes differ strongly among groups or temperatures, checking whether conclusions change under both definitions. In practice, there will often be little difference between relative and absolute summaries (i.e., *z* and $CT_{max}$), but this distinction is important nonetheless.

Equivalently, the model can be reparameterised to estimate *z* and $CT_{max}$ directly. Inverting the relations above ($\beta_1 = -1/z$ and $\beta_0 = \log_{10} t_\text{ref} + (CT_{max} - \bar{T})/z$) reparameterises the midpoint as $\text{mid}(T) = \log_{10} t_\text{ref} + \bigl(CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) - T\bigr)/z$, which substituted into @eq-4pl gives

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\left(k\,\left[\log_{10} t - \log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \tfrac{CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) - T}{z}\right]\right)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl-direct}

Now *z* (estimated on the log scale so it stays positive) and $CT_{max}$ are estimated parameters in their own right, rather than quantities recovered from @eq-mid-temp after fitting. @eq-4pl-direct uses the relative threshold (each curve's own midpoint); an absolute threshold is obtained by adding the midpoint-scale asymmetry correction of @eq-t50, $\frac{1}{k}\log\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}$, to $\text{mid}(T)$ before solving for $CT_{max}$. Again, temperature effects (and any interactions with moderators) should be included on $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, and $k$ to correctly estimate absolute thresholds, but are only needed on $\text{mid}$ for relative thresholds.

A powerful aspect of the modelling approach described above is that uncertainty in all estimated quantities can be carried through the full analysis. The direct parameterisation (@eq-4pl-direct) opens up ways of modelling *z* and $CT_{max}$ that the two-stage approach cannot, while ensuring correct error propagation and inference. Each parameter in the model — `low`, `up`, `k`, `mid`, and, under @eq-4pl-direct, *z* and $CT_{max}$ — can be estimated separately for biologically meaningful strata (e.g., sex, population, species, treatment) by adding covariates and, where appropriate, random effects to the relevant parameter predictors (see @eq-empirical-direct below). This makes *a priori* tests of key hypotheses (e.g., do sexes or species differ significantly in *z* and $CT_{max}$?) and variance decomposition (e.g., how much variation in *z* and $CT_{max}$ arises across populations, species, or trials?) part of the model rather than a post-hoc comparison of fitted curves, all with correct inference. We fit the examples below in a Bayesian framework because posterior draws make uncertainty propagation straightforward, but the same likelihoods can also be fit in a frequentist framework, with uncertainty propagated by profiled likelihoods, the Delta method or parametric bootstrap.

# Predicting heat injury and survival under dynamic thermal environments

A particularly powerful aspect of TDT/TLS models are their ability to translate dynamic temperature time series from nature to predict heat injury (HI) accumulation and thus population survival probability [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @li2023; @orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. Heat injury accumulates as the time spent at a given temperature divided by the median lethal time at that temperature, integrated across the temperature trajectory $T(i)$ as follows:

$$
HI(t) \;=\; 100 \int_0^t 10^{\,\big(T(i)\,-\,CT_{max_{1hr}}\big)/z} \,di,
$$ {#eq-hi}

where $z$ describes thermal sensitivity and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ is the static temperature at which 50% of the population reaches the assayed endpoint after one hour of exposure. Larger $z$ values indicate that a larger temperature increase is needed to reduce tolerance time by a factor of 10, so they correspond to lower sensitivity of tolerance time to warming. When $HI(t) = 100\%$, the cumulative dose equals one $t_{50}$ at the 1-hour reference, predicting 50% mortality (or 50% loss of the assayed sublethal endpoint, e.g.\ fertility; @orsted_suzukii_2024). Injury accumulates only when $T(i)$ exceeds a critical temperature $T_{crit}$ below which damage is repaired as fast as it accrues [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022].

A more natural prediction to make may instead be the population-level survival probability over time [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. This has the advantage of allowing for a direct connection to population dynamical models which typically use survival, growth and fecundity as vital rates for population-level predictions [@noble2026]. To predict survival, we can calculate the *effective exposure time* at a chosen reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$. More effective exposure time is accumulated at higher temperatures and less at cool ones, in proportion to the scaling factor $10^{(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$,

$$
\tau_{\text{eff}}(t) \;=\; \int_0^t 10^{\,(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,di,
$$ {#eq-tau-eff}

We can then evaluate the 4PL surface once at the cumulative effective exposure to obtain the predicted population-level survival fraction at every point in the trajectory:

$$
S_{\text{pred}}(t) \;=\; S\!\big(T_{\text{ref}},\, \tau_{\text{eff}}(t)\big).
$$ {#eq-S-pred}

Here $S(T, \tau)$ is the survival surface fitted by the dose-response model, so $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ is the survival probability for an organism held at *constant* $T_{\text{ref}}$ after exposure duration $\tau_{\text{eff}}(t)$. Direct error propagation of $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (using either Bayesian or frequentist approaches) allows one to easily model variation in outcomes, which can be helpful in making informed predictions in nature.

# Simulations - Evaluating statistical properties of the joint and two-stage modelling approaches

We assessed the performance of both the 4PL and two-stage approaches by estimating bias and 95% coverage for *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ across simulation replicates. Bias was calculated as the estimated value minus the true value, whereas coverage was defined as the proportion of intervals containing the true parameter value. The 4PL approach estimated parameter uncertainty directly from the fitted model. In contrast, the two-stage approach required uncertainty to be propagated from the Stage-2 linear model using the Delta method, a first-order Taylor approximation for nonlinear functions of estimated parameters [@bolker_ecologicalmodels_2008; @lappi2018]. For the two-stage estimates, we constructed intervals using both normal and *t*-distribution quantiles to evaluate the sensitivity of coverage to different assumptions about sampling uncertainty.

We tested the performance of the two-stage and 4PL approaches across scenarios designed to separate different sources of estimator bias and uncertainty. First, we simulated a strict-equivalence baseline in which survival spanned the full 0–1 range, there was no overdispersion, and only the dose-response midpoint varied with temperature. This scenario tested whether the two approaches recover the same quantities when the assumptions of the classical two-stage model are met. Second, we introduced beta-binomial overdispersion while keeping the same underlying dose-response shape, allowing us to isolate the cost of likelihood misspecification when binomial models are applied to overdispersed count data. Third, we simulated departures from the classical dose-response assumptions by allowing the upper asymptote to decline with temperature, both asymptotes to compress with temperature, or the curve steepness to change with temperature. These scenarios reflect empirical cases in which survival or sublethal responses do not span the full response range, or where the shape of the dose-response curve changes across assay temperatures [e.g., @bullard2026]. We also ran sensitivity analyses that varied the strength of upper-asymptote drift and the overall level of the upper asymptote.

Finally, we evaluated how different experimental designs impact estimates by simulating response curves from several static temperatures and exposure durations with modest numbers of individuals, samples, or replicate vials per treatment [@truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @faber_tdt_plants_2024]. In the simulations, designs included 1, 3, or 5 replicate vials per temperature-by-time treatment, with 10 to 20 organisms per vial sampled from a uniform distribution. We also explored experimental designs that conducted experiments over short time spans across the different temperatures which we expected should impact model fit and therefore *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. For each scenario-by-design combination, we simulated 1,000 datasets and fitted both the two-stage (both relative and absolute summaries) and 4PL approaches. Overall, this amounted to 29,000 simulations across the 29 scenario-by-design combinations. We report Monte Carlo standard errors (MCSE) for the bias and coverage summaries to better understand across simulation uncertainty [@williams2024; @direnzo2023; @koehler2009].

#### *Simulation results*

When the data-generating process matched the classical assumptions, with survival spanning the full 0–1 range and temperature affecting only the dose-response midpoint, the two-stage approach performed similarly to the joint 4PL model, producing little bias and near-nominal coverage when confidence intervals were constructed with *t*-distribution quantiles. Using normal (z) quantiles instead produced anti-conservative intervals with coverage well below the nominal 95% level ([Supplement 8.1](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#strict-equivalence-baseline) & [8.2](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#likelihood-misspecification-alone)).

In contrast, the two-stage approach resulted in biased *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, often with poor coverage, when temperature affected the upper asymptote (`up`) or the rate of decline (`k`) (@fig-sims). Bias for both *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from the two-stage approaches was particularly severe as the upper survival threshold differed from 1, or decreased with temperature (@fig-sims2). These biases were consistent even with larger sample sizes ([Supplement 8.6](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#experimental-design-impacts)), but they became most consequential under exactly the types of experimental designs used in practice: sparse temperature-duration grids, short exposure windows, and low replication (@fig-sim-design). The joint 4PL remained near-unbiased and well-calibrated across these designs, whereas two-stage bias grew and interval coverage collapsed as grids became sparser, replication reduced, or exposure windows shortened — most severely at the shortest exposure cap, where two-stage coverage of *z* fell below 0.05 even with the *t*-correction (@fig-sim-design; full per-cell detail in [Supplement 8.6](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#experimental-design-impacts)).

```{r}
#| label: fig-sims
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 9.5
#| fig-cap: "Simulation performance when the data-generating process violates classical TDT shape assumptions. Row labelled **'up varies with T'**: upper asymptote $\\text{up}$ declines 0.01 per °C from 0.92 at $T=34$ °C, with $\\text{low}=0.05$ and $k=8$ constant in $T$. Row labelled **'k varies with T'**: slope $k$ ranges from 7 at 30 °C to 9 at 38 °C, with $\\text{up}=0.92$ and $\\text{low}=0.05$ constant. Both rows use 5 replicate vials per (T × duration) cell, $N_{sim}=1000$ simulated datasets per row, and beta-binomial overdispersion ($\\phi=5$). Panel a): mean signed bias in $z$ (left column) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (right column) with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE). Panel b): 95% interval coverage with the same column layout. Dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal coverage. Two-stage Normal- and t-quantile variants share point estimates and differ only in Stage-2 delta-method interval construction. 4PL = four-parameter log-logistic, BB = beta-binomial, bin = binomial, CI = credible interval."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + paired colour palette (matches notes writeup) --
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bin_t",
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

# Use n_reps = 5 cells of Scen 3 and Scen 5 for the manuscript figure.
.d <- dplyr::bind_rows(.load_screened("scen3"),
                       .load_screened("scen5")) |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("_n5$", scenario)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                labels = .method_labels),
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                labels = c("z (°C)",
                                           "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    scenario_label = dplyr::recode(
      sub("_n5$", "", scenario),
      scen3_heat_lowers_u = "up varies with T",
      scen5_sharpen       = "k varies with T"),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
    cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
  )

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.qlab2 <- as_labeller(c(`z (°C)` = "italic(z)~(degree*C)",
                        `CT[max,1h] (°C)` = "CT[max[1*hr]]~(degree*C)"), label_parsed)

.p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity, scales = "free_x", labeller = labeller(quantity = .qlab2)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL,
       title = "a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")

.p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity, labeller = labeller(quantity = .qlab2)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
  labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
       title = "b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

.p_bias / .p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-sims2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-cap: "Bias in $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ under two departures from a full upper asymptote. Panel a) (upper asymptote varies): $\\beta_{up} = 0$ (no temperature-dependent shift), but the upper asymptote is held at a reduced level across all temperatures, so the maximum attainable survival is lower throughout the grid. Panel b) (upper asymptote shifts with temperature): the upper asymptote at $T = \\bar T$ is fixed at $\\text{up}_0 = 0.92$ while its per-°C shift ($\\beta_{up}$) is varied, so $\\text{up}$ stays high at cool assays and declines progressively at hotter ones. Both panels: $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, mid linear in $T$ with slope $-0.15$, beta-binomial likelihood ($\\phi=5$), $n_{reps}=5$, $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. Points are mean signed bias with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE); dashed lines mark zero bias. Left column: $z$; right column: $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. Only the four distinct point-estimate methods are shown: the two-stage Normal- and t-quantile variants share point estimates within each Stage-1 family, so bias is identical between them. Coverage differences are presented in the supplement for [panel a](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#coverage-for-constant-but-reduced-up-textup_0) and [panel b](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#coverage-for-drifting-up-across-temperatures-beta_up)."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + colour palette ---------------------------------
# Bias is identical for the Normal-CI and t-CI variants of each
# two-stage Stage-1 family (they share point estimates; only Stage-2 CI
# construction differs). For this bias-only figure we therefore plot
# just the four distinct point-estimate methods: two_stage_bin,
# two_stage_bb, joint_4pl (relative), joint_4pl_abs (absolute).
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bb",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin", "Two-stage BB",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)", "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f", "#a63603", "#006d2c", "#a1d99b"),
                         .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

.scen6 <- .load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 10,
                sweep_label = "up shifts with T (varying beta_up, percentage points / degC)")

.scen7 <- .load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100,
                sweep_label = "up is constant but reduced (varying up_0)")

.shape_fmt <- function(d) {
  d |>
    dplyr::filter(method %in% .method_levels) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                  labels = c("z (°C)",
                                             "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
      bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias
    )
}

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.qlab2 <- as_labeller(c(`z (°C)` = "italic(z)~(degree*C)",
                        `CT[max,1h] (°C)` = "CT[max[1*hr]]~(degree*C)"), label_parsed)

.build <- function(d, x_label, x_breaks) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = sweep_x, y = mean_bias, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = bias_lo, ymax = bias_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1, labeller = .qlab2) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)") +
    .theme_proj
}

.p6 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen6),
              x_label  = "Temperature-dependent shift in upper asymptote (percentage points/°C)",
              x_breaks = c(-1.9, -1.5, -1.0, -0.5)) +
  ggtitle("b) Upper asymptote shifts with temperature")

.p7 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen7),
              x_label  = "Upper asymptote value",
              x_breaks = c(0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99)) +
  ggtitle("a) Upper asymptote varies")

.p7 / .p6 + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-design
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Estimator performance across experimental designs. Panels a) and b) vary experimental design and replication: a full (5 temperatures × 6 durations) versus sparse (3 × 4) temperature–duration grid, crossed with 1, 3, or 5 replicate vials per cell. Panels c) and d) vary the maximum exposure duration (the longest assay window: 60, 120, 240, or 405 min) on the full grid with five replicates. Panels a) and c) show mean signed bias and panels b) and d) show 95% interval coverage for thermal sensitivity $z$ (left) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (right). Points are Monte Carlo means with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE); dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal 0.95 coverage. Bias panels show the four distinct point estimators (the two-stage Normal- and *t*-CI variants share point estimates); coverage panels additionally distinguish the *t*-CI variants. All cells use a beta-binomial data-generating process ($\\phi=5$) with a fixed 4PL shape ($\\text{up}=0.92$, $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, midpoint linear in $T$); $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. Full per-cell detail and RMSE are in [the supplement](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#experimental-design-impacts)."

# Combined experimental-design figure (scen8 = design × replication; scen9 =
# maximum exposure duration). Reads the per-simulation aggregates only — no
# model fits here. Mirrors the screening/aggregation used by the other
# simulation figures.
library(dplyr); library(tidyr); library(ggplot2); library(here); library(patchwork)

.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin","two_stage_bin_t","two_stage_bb",
                    "two_stage_bb_t","joint_4pl","joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)","Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)","Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)","Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)
.point_codes <- c("two_stage_bin","two_stage_bb","joint_4pl","joint_4pl_abs")
.TRUTH_REL   <- c(z = -1 / -0.15, CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(here::here("output","sim_twostage"),
                      pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
                      full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))
  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z" & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |> dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)
  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario","sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb","two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                                     .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)], truth),
                  bias = estimate - truth_use,
                  covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                            lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(n = dplyr::n(),
                     mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
                     mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
                     coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
                     mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered) * (1 - mean(covered)) / dplyr::n()),
                     .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::mutate(method_code = as.character(method))
}

.fmt <- function(d) d |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z","CTmax_1hr"),
                      labels = c("z (°C)","CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    method_lab = factor(.method_labels[match(method_code, .method_levels)],
                        levels = .method_labels),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias, bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,  cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov)

.d8 <- .fmt(.load_screened("scen8")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(design = ifelse(grepl("full", scenario), "full", "sparse"),
                nrep = as.integer(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                cell = factor(sprintf("%s × %d", design, nrep),
                              levels = rev(c("sparse × 1","full × 1","sparse × 3",
                                             "full × 3","sparse × 5","full × 5"))))
.d9 <- .fmt(.load_screened("scen9")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(tmax = as.integer(sub(".*_tmax_([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                cell = factor(sprintf("%d min", tmax),
                              levels = rev(c("60 min","120 min","240 min","405 min"))))

.qlab <- as_labeller(c(`z (°C)` = "italic(z)~(degree*C)",
                       `CT[max,1h] (°C)` = "CT[max[1*hr]]~(degree*C)"), label_parsed)
.thm <- theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(), strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.title = element_blank(), legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
        panel.spacing = unit(0.6, "lines"))
.scol <- scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols, breaks = .method_labels,
                             limits = .method_labels, drop = FALSE)

.bias_panel <- function(d, title)
  ggplot(dplyr::filter(d, method_code %in% .point_codes),
         aes(mean_bias, cell, colour = method_lab, group = method_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = .3,
                 position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.7, position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x", labeller = .qlab) +
  .scol + guides(colour = "none") +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL, title = title) + .thm
.cov_panel <- function(d, title)
  ggplot(d, aes(coverage, cell, colour = method_lab, group = method_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = .95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = .3,
                 position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.7, position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, labeller = .qlab) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, .5, .95)) +
  .scol + guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                       override.aes = list(size = 2.6))) +
  labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL, title = title) + .thm

.fig_design <-
  (.bias_panel(.d8, "a) Design × replication — bias") |
   .cov_panel(.d8,  "b) Design × replication — coverage")) /
  (.bias_panel(.d9, "c) Maximum exposure duration — bias") |
   .cov_panel(.d9,  "d) Maximum exposure duration — coverage")) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")

dir.create(here::here("output","figs"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
ggsave(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.png"), .fig_design,
       width = 11, height = 10, dpi = 600, bg = "white")
ggsave(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.svg"), .fig_design,
       width = 11, height = 10, bg = "white")
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.png"))
```

# Worked examples

We applied the 4PL modelling framework to four case studies spanning lethal counts, continuous-proportion physiological data and sublethal responses: three cereal-aphid species [@li2023], snow gum leaf PSII function [@arnold_psii_2026], zebrafish larvae under varying oxygen levels [@saruhashi2026] and vinegar fly mortality, heat coma and productivity [@orsted_suzukii_2024]. These examples show how the same fitted response surface can estimate $z$, $CT_{max}$ and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$, compare groups, and propagate uncertainty into heat-injury and survival predictions. The workflow is implemented in `bayesTLS`; the online supplement provides installation instructions, a tutorial and the full model specifications ([Supplement Section 2.2](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-empirical-model-specs)).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mid}_{ij}(T) &= \log_{10} t_{\text{ref}} + \frac{CT_{max,ij}(t_{\text{ref}}) - T_{ij}}{z_{ij}},\\
CT_{max,ij}(t_{\text{ref}}) &= \alpha_0 + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + b_j,\\
\log z_{ij} &= \zeta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\zeta} + u_j .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-empirical-direct}

For the main grouped case studies, we used the direct parameterisation in @eq-empirical-direct, placing species, oxygen treatment or recovery condition directly on $CT_{max}$ and $z$. This makes group-specific thermal limits and sensitivities model parameters rather than post-hoc summaries. The vinegar-fly sex comparison was fit in the equivalent midpoint form for demonstration, with `sex` and its temperature interaction on $\text{mid}$. Count responses were fit with beta-binomial likelihoods, while proportions in the snow gum example were fit with a beta likelihood. We fitted all models with the Bayesian implementation, using `brms` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("brms"))`; @burkner_brms_2017) and Stan (v`r tryCatch(cmdstanr::cmdstan_version(), error = function(e) "2.38.0")`; @carpenter2017) through `bayesTLS` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("bayesTLS"))`). Equivalent frequentist models are available through `freqTLS` and should give similar point estimates for the same likelihood and parameterisation.

For the lethal aphid and vinegar-fly examples, we also used posterior draws to predict heat injury and survival under field or field-derived temperature traces, with and without an illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield temperature-dependent repair function (sensu @arnold_thermal_2025). Full formulas, priors, sampler controls, diagnostics and temperature-trace details are reported in the [online supplement](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-empirical-model-specs).

```{r}
#| label: aphid-hi-facts
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# Read cached aphid heat-injury facts, with fallbacks for independent rendering.
.f5 <- tryCatch(readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig5_hi_data.rds")),
                error = function(e) NULL)
.f5_fresh <- !is.null(.f5$aphid_Tc)
# Summarise the per-species T_crit range.
aphid_Tc_rng <- if (.f5_fresh) range(.f5$aphid_Tc$Tc) else c(23.8, 28.2)
# Summarise end-of-season survival without repair.
aphid_surv_end <- if (.f5_fresh) {
  .f5$aphid |>
    dplyr::filter(repair == "No repair") |>
    dplyr::group_by(species) |>
    dplyr::slice_max(time, n = 1) |>
    dplyr::pull(surv_med)
} else c(0.008, 0.019)
```

The survival estimates use posterior draws for $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and $T_{crit}$ (estimated as the temperature at which injury starts to accumulate), linking laboratory tolerance to predictions of survival in the field.

## Cereal aphids (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*)

@li2023 found pronounced differences in heat tolerance and sensitivity among species, which likely explain changes in cereal-aphid communities. Our 4PL model recovers comparable findings, with *R. padi* having the highest $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (`r aphid_ct_rp` °C, exceeding *M. dirhodum* by `r ci1(cc$aphid_ct_rp_md)` °C, `r pmc(cc$aphid_ct_rp_md)`) and *M. dirhodum* the shallowest thermal-death-time slope (largest $z$, `r aphid_z_md` °C) and the lowest $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ ([@fig-tls-comparison]a). Modelling these data with the 4PL provides clear benefits for understanding differences between species because the comparison comes directly from a single model. Using field temperatures from the same site (Wuhan, China), we also show how the 4PL can predict field mortality with uncertainty propagated from that same model: survival without repair is predicted to fall to roughly `r round(100 * min(aphid_surv_end))`-`r round(100 * max(aphid_surv_end))`% by late June, while physiological repair helped maintain survival at `r rep1(cc$aphid_repair$R_padi)`% and `r rep1(cc$aphid_repair$S_avenae)`% for *R. padi* and *S. avenae*, but we still predicted `r rep1(cc$aphid_repair$M_dirhodum)`% survival for *M. dirhodum*, the least-tolerant species ([@fig-heat-injury]d-f). The model therefore moves inference from ranking laboratory tolerance to predicting which species is most likely to experience mortality during natural fluctuating temperature regimes.

## Snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*)

@arnold_psii_2026 showed that light conditions alter estimates of heat damage to photosystem II (PSII), an ecologically important endpoint because snow gum leaves experience extreme heat during daylight. By fitting a 4PL model to continuous PSII proportions (post/pre $F_v/F_m$) with a beta distribution, we reproduce the key result that light conditions following heat stress lower heat tolerance, while clarifying the mechanism at play: light treatment reduced $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ by `r ci1a(cc$sg_ct)` °C (`r pmc(cc$sg_ct)`), but did not affect $z$ (`r pmc(cc$sg_z)`; [@fig-tls-comparison]b) relative to the dark treatment. Therefore, light mainly lowers the thermal load required to impair PSII, but not the rate at which such load accumulates over time, implying that daylight heatwaves may push leaves into heat stress at lower temperatures than dark-recovery assays suggest.

## Zebrafish (*Danio rerio*)

@saruhashi2026 found that oxygen modulates heat tolerance in zebrafish larvae, an important result because freshwater heatwaves often coincide with changing oxygen availability. We show that the 4PL recovers higher heat tolerance under hyperoxia ($CT_{max_{1hr}}$ `r zf_ct_hyper_ci` vs `r zf_ct_norm_ci` °C; [@fig-tls-comparison]c), but also that oxygen *steepens* the thermal-death-time relationship relative to normoxia ($\Delta z$ `r ci1(cc$zf_z)` °C, `r pmc(cc$zf_z)`). The 4PL therefore provides stronger ecological inference than conventional two-stage approaches: oxygen does not simply raise heat tolerance, it also changes how fast injury is predicted to accumulate during heat stress, so its benefit depends on exposure duration as well as temperature intensity.

## Vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*)

@orsted_suzukii_2024 found that heat coma and declines in productivity occur at temperatures below survival limits but follow traditional TDT relationships, implying that sublethal effects have large implications for population dynamics. Our re-analysis of their survival data recovers similar variation in thermal tolerance and sensitivity between sexes ($z$ `r fly_z_f_ci` °C for females and `r fly_z_m_ci` °C for males; $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ ≈ `r fly_ct_4h` °C; [@fig-tls-comparison]d), but estimates the sex contrast directly in a unified model and finds no detectable difference ($\Delta z$ `r ci1(cc$fly_z)` °C, `r pmc(cc$fly_z)`; $CT_{max}$ `r pmc(cc$fly_ct)`). In the supplement, we extend the same logic to heat coma and productivity, including a hurdle model that separates reproductive failure from changes in offspring number among reproducing flies ([coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [productivity](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses). We also generated predictions from microclimate temperature-time series for Rennes, France, to illustrate predicted mortality under realistic extreme-heat exposure and the importance of physiological repair ([@fig-heat-injury]a-c). The 4PL model therefore reveals not only when organisms die, but also which vital rates fail first under extreme heat.

```{r}
#| label: fig-tls-comparison
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Thermal sensitivity ($z$, left of each panel) and the critical thermal limit ($CT_{max}$, right) estimated by the joint Bayesian four-parameter log-logistic (4PL) model across four case studies: a) three cereal-aphid species (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*; @li2023), b) snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) leaf photosystem II function under dark vs 90-min light treatment during recovery from heat stress [@arnold_psii_2026], c) zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) larvae under normoxia and hyperoxia conditions [@saruhashi2026], and d) vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) males and females [@orsted_suzukii_2024]. Larger $z$ values indicate lower sensitivity of tolerance time to warming (i.e., a larger increase in temperature is required to reduce tolerance time by a factor of 10). Filled ridges are posterior densities, coloured by group. Points and error bars correspond to posterior medians and 95% credible intervals, which are also printed beside each distribution. The $CT_{max}$ is predicted for an exposure duration of 1 hour for all case studies except vinegar flies, where we used 4 hours as per the original case study. Within each panel, grouping variables were fit within a single joint model, so that differences among groups are evaluated as pairwise posterior contrasts, annotated as brackets labelled with $p_{\\mathrm{MCMC}}$."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig4_case_study_densities.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-heat-injury
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Heat-injury accumulation and predicted survival under field temperature-time series for the vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*, by sex; panels a-c) and three cereal aphids (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*; panels d-f). (a, d) Temperature-time series: NicheMapR [@kearney2017] microclimate temperatures in the shade for the vinegar fly (Rennes, France, Jun–Aug 2018; @orsted_suzukii_2024) and hourly air temperatures for the aphid site (Wuhan, May–June 2016; @li2023 site and period, re-sourced from the Open-Meteo ERA5 reanalysis), each with the critical temperature threshold $T_{crit}$ (dotted line). (b, e) Cumulative heat injury; 100% corresponds to one $LT_{50}$ dose, i.e. the thermal dose predicted to cause a 50% decline in survival (dotted grey line). (c, f) Predicted survival. Both case studies show predictions without (solid line) or with (dashed line) physiological repair at permissive temperatures. Fly panels are coloured by sex, aphid panels by species. Bands are ±1 SE across posterior draws. Repair uses an illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield kernel with $r_{ref}=0.03$ LT50-dose h$^{-1}$ at the reference temperature; this repair function is illustrative and was not derived empirically."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig5_heat_injury.png"))
```

# Discussion

Our results show that TDT/TLS analyses do not need to trade biological realism for statistical coherence. The re-parameterized 4PL model provides a flexible and statistically rigorous framework for estimating thermal tolerance and thermal sensitivity, recovering familiar quantities such as $z$, $CT_{max}$ and $T_{crit}$. At the same time, it estimates these measures from the full temperature-duration response surface and propagates uncertainty into every derived quantity. Simulations show that the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold, but is more reliable when experiments are sparse, replication is low, exposure windows are short, or the response bounds (`low`, `up`) depart from 0 and 1. The case studies also recover biologically plausible TLS estimates, with $z$ values falling within the range typically reported for thermal tolerance landscapes (approximately 2-7 °C; @rezende_landscapes_2014), while extending inference to group contrasts, heat-injury prediction and sublethal endpoints.

A crucial new undertaking in a warming world is to better understand the ecological and evolutionary drivers of thermal sensitivity and vulnerability across life. Species, populations, life stages or treatments differ in the temperature at which damage begins, the rate at which tolerance time declines with warming, the steepness of the response curve, baseline survival, maximum attainable survival, or the capacity for repair. We need new statistical approaches that make quantifying these differences easier and more robust. The case studies illustrate the power of the joint modelling approach. In cereal aphids, the model links interspecific differences in laboratory tolerance to predicted survival under a field temperature trace and was able to more formally compare differences among species statistically. In the snow gum, the joint model showed that light lowered the heat threshold ($CT_{max}$) for PSII impairment without detectably changing thermal sensitivity ($z$), while accounting for the covariance between the two. In zebrafish, we show that hyperoxia (relative to normoxia) shifts the upper thermal limit and may also change the shape of the temperature-duration landscape. In vinegar flies, it connects mortality, coma and productivity to the broader question of which vital rates fail first under heat. Such ecological inferences about vulnerability provide new insights into the mechanisms underlying population risk, not only alternative ways for estimating $z$ or $CT_{max}$.

Because the model fits raw response data in a single hierarchical analysis, it also creates new opportunities for asking where variation in vulnerability comes from. Clones, individuals, tissues, populations or treatments can be included in the same model to estimate genetic, individual, tissue-specific or environmental sources of variation in $z$ and $CT_{max}$. The underlying 4PL parameters (`low`, `up`, `k`, and `mid`) can also be contrasted to determine whether groups differ in baseline survival, maximum attainable survival, curve steepness or time-to-threshold. This kind of decomposition is difficult with a two-stage pipeline because the first stage collapses the response surface to a small set of point estimates and the second stage treats those estimates as data. Full propagation of sampling uncertainty should make sensitivity and tolerance estimates more useful for robust meta-analyses, comparative studies and systems models of biological responses to extreme heat [@noble2026].

The same logic extends beyond lethal count data to many sublethal measures that are of interest to ecologists and evolutionary biologists and could arguably be more important in assessing vulnerability [@snook_fertility_2026; @arnold2026; @orsted2026]. We show how related models can be used for time-to-event responses, mixture models that separate response and non-response components, Gamma-distributed responses and continuous proportions, including the vinegar-fly [coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [productivity](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses. More generally, any dose-response process in which biological response depends jointly on stress intensity and exposure duration could be analysed in this framework, including acclimation experiments, interacting environmental stressors and toxicology studies where toxicant concentration replaces temperature [@arnold_thermal_2025]. This opens a route to reporting sensitivity and tolerance in a consistent way across fields.

While the 4PL framework is powerful, it also has limitations. The 4PL is parameter rich, so very small datasets may estimate some parameters poorly and models may at times need to be simplified. Our simulations indicate that, with appropriate priors, it can still recover correctly calibrated estimates under small sample sizes and less rigorous designs. Bayesian implementations also require priors that are appropriate for the biological system, although `bayesTLS` is designed to make these models more accessible to experimental and comparative biologists. Similar models can also be fit in a likelihood framework, with uncertainty propagated using profiled likelihood, parametric bootstrapping or the Delta method [@bolker_ecologicalmodels_2008], and we provide this option through `freqTLS`. However, propagating uncertainty into survival and heat-injury predictions is more straightforward in the Bayesian framework. A major next step is to estimate repair directly from experiments [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @orsted2026], because our illustrative Sharpe-Schoolfield repair model shows that predictions can change substantially when repair is included. Future models that combine heat injury, repair, acclimation, behaviour and microclimate validation will be essential for translating laboratory tolerance surfaces into robust forecasts in nature [@faber2026a].

Overall, the 4PL framework turns thermal tolerance from a static threshold into a dynamic, uncertainty-aware forecast of vulnerability. By linking laboratory response surfaces to realistic temperature time series, it helps identify not only which species are close to their limits, but when, where and through which biological processes extreme heat is most likely to erode fitness. This shift is essential to predict vulnerability in a warming world where extreme heat will become a more common occurrence.

# Acknowledgements

We thank Johannes Overgaard, Joey Bernhardt, Wilco Verbeck, Jennifer Sunday, Ignacio Peralta-Maraver, Arnaud Sentis and Jeff Arnoldi for discussions during an extreme heat workshop supported by the ClimateCountDown ERC Consolidator Grant, funded by the European Research Council under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (Grant agreement no. 101169909). We would also like to thank Gwenaëlle Deconninck, Charlie Cornwallis and Tobias Uller for helpful comments. D.W.A.N was supported by an ARC Future Fellowship (FT220100276) and thanks the ANU, Lund University, the ANU Hansen Scandinavian Friendship Grant and the Royal Physiographic Society of Lund for funding that supported the sabbatical during which this paper was written. P.P. was supported by a Wenner-Gren Stiftelserna postdoctoral fellowship (UPD2024-0239).

# Author contributions {.unnumbered}

D.W.A.N.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.P.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.A.A.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – Review & Editing. S.N.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Resources, Writing – Review & Editing. All authors contributed to interpretation of results, reviewed and edited the manuscript, approved the final version, and agree to be accountable for the work.

# Data and code availability {.unnumbered}

Analysis scripts, case-study data, committed model fits, and rendered outputs are available at https://github.com/daniel1noble/bayesTLS. Cached simulation outputs and model fits are archived on OSF (https://osf.io/c6dxy) and can be fetched with `make data`; the raw simulation files are regenerable from the simulation scripts in the repository. The online supplement, tutorial, and `bayesTLS` installation instructions are available at https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/. We also provide a sister package, `freqTLS`, which develops these models in a likelihood framework.

# Conflict of interest {.unnumbered}

The authors declare no competing interests.

# AI Declaration
During the preparation of this manuscript, the authors used generative AI tools, including OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex (vers. 5.5) and Claude Code (vers. opus 4.8), to assist with language editing, structural refinement, mathematical derivations, code building and checking, R package construction and formatting. All scientific ideas, analyses, interpretations, and conclusions were developed and verified by the authors. Any AI-assisted output was critically reviewed and edited by the authors, who accept full responsibility for the final content of the manuscript, supplement, code, and associated R packages.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
